what: Sparse models and the tuning problem
title: The PIC paradigm
text: sparse estimators solve penalized problem form \hat{\boldsymbol\beta}(\lambda) \;=\; \arg\min_{\boldsymbol\beta}\; L(\boldsymbol\beta) \;+\; \lambda \, \mathrm{pen}(\boldsymbol\beta), L measures fit \mathrm{pen} shrinks coefficients toward zero. single knob \lambda \ge 0 controls many variables survive: larger \lambda means sparser model. Everything hinges \lambda chosen. default answer practice cross-validation (CV). CV optimizes --sample prediction error - target recovering true set active variables. model can predict well including many spurious predictors, CV-optimal \lambda typically far small selection, letting noise variables . selection-versus- prediction distinction well documented model-selection literature.

title: The PIC paradigm
text: PIC takes different route: fixes \lambda detection boundary: smallest penalty level , absence signal, leaves gradient fit term penalty therefore returns empty model. Equivalently, smallest \lambda makes origin \boldsymbol\beta = \mathbf 0 local minimum penalized objective null H_0:\boldsymbol\beta = \mathbf 0. Formally, consider gradient-norm statistic (transformed) loss origin, \Lambda \;=\; \left\| \nabla_{\boldsymbol\beta}\,(\phi \circ \ell_n)\bigl(g(\hat\beta_0 \mathbf 1), \hat\sigma; (X, Y_0)\bigr) \right\|_\infty,  Y_0 drawn H_0 nuisance parameters set maximum-likelihood estimates. detection boundary upper \alpha-quantile \Lambda, \lambda_\alpha^{\mathrm{DB}} \;=\; q_{1-\alpha}(\Lambda), , construction, \mathbb P_{H_0}(\hat{\boldsymbol\beta} = \mathbf 0) = 1 - \alpha. words: choose smallest \lambda keeps pure noise selected probability 1-\alpha. level \alpha (default 0.05) plays role nominal false-discovery level null. Choosing \lambda < \lambda_\alpha^{\rm PDB} floods model false positives, whereas \lambda > \lambda_\alpha^{\rm PDB} needlessly misses true signals.

title: The PIC paradigm
text: transformations (\phi, g) - family-specific built picreg - chosen precisely distribution \Lambda (asymptotically) pivotal: free unknown nuisance parameters (intercept \beta_0 scale \sigma). consequence quantile \lambda_\alpha^{\mathrm{PDB}} — pivotal detection boundary — depends design matrix X, family, level \alpha, never observed response \mathbf y. can therefore computed fitting (reused across penalties). choice calibrated selection rather prediction, PIC typically achieves sharper support recovery CV-tuned competitors - , standard sparsity assumptions, sharp phase transition exact recovery, phenomenon well documented compressed-sensing literature. companion paper, Sardy, van Cutsem van de Geer (2026), gives table listing base loss \ell_n associated pair (\phi, g) six families.

title: The PIC paradigm
text: picreg offers three ways obtain quantile \Lambda, via lambda_method argument pic(): "mc_exact" (default): family-aware Monte Carlo — accurate, ideal small moderate problems. "mc_gaussian": CLT-based Gaussian approximation gradient, essentially equivalent n moderately large noticeably faster. "analytical": closed-form Bonferroni bound, deterministic O(1), large-scale problems. diagnostic pdb_asymptotic() lets visualize three agree n grows.

title: An introduction to `picreg`
text: picreg R implementation Pivotal Information Criterion (PIC) developed Sardy, van Cutsem, van de Geer https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.04172. PIC general framework improve BIC LASSO fitting sparse regression linear models regularization parameter \lambda selected automatically pivotal statistic. result, PIC removes need cross-validation achieves accurate support recovery better identifying true non-zero coefficients. package fits resulting estimators across six response distributions - Gaussian, binomial, Poisson, exponential, Gumbel, Cox - combined three sparsity-inducing penalties; \ell_1 (LASSO), Smoothly Clipped Absolute Deviation (SCAD), Minimax Concave Penalty (MCP). Given data \mathcal{D} = (X, y), X \\mathbb{R}^{n \times p} denotes design matrix y \\mathbb{R}^n response vector, base loss l_n(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \sigma; \mathcal{D}) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{=1}^n l(\theta_i, \sigma; \mathcal{D}) (possible nuisance parameter \sigma), sparsity-inducing penalty P (possibly non-convex), pic() minimizes Pivotal Information Criterion (PIC) solving  \min_{\beta_0,\,\boldsymbol{\beta}}\left\{   \phi\bigl(l_n(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \sigma; \mathcal{D})\bigr)   + \lambda_\alpha^{\mathrm{PDB}}\, P(\boldsymbol{\beta})\right\}, \qquad \boldsymbol{\theta} = g\bigl(\beta_0\mathbf{1} + X\boldsymbol{\beta}\bigr),  \lambda_\alpha^{\mathrm{PDB}} pre-fixed parameter \lambda chosen upper \alpha-quantile statistic \Lambda made pivotal respect unknown parameters \sigma,\, \beta_0 thanks distribution-specific pair (\phi,\, g). Internally, picreg uses FISTA (Fast Iterative Soft-Thresholding Algorithm) optimization scheme.

title: An introduction to `picreg`
text: "gaussian" default family argument pic(). objective function Gaussian distribution uses MSE l_n \phi(\cdot) = \sqrt{\cdot}, g(\cdot) = \mathrm{Id} transformations, resulting square-root LASSO objective  \min_{\beta_0,\,\boldsymbol{\beta}}\left\{\sqrt{\frac1n\sum_{=1}^n\left(y_i-(\beta_0+\mathbf x_i^T\boldsymbol\beta\right)^2}   + \lambda_\alpha^{\mathrm{PDB}}\, P(\boldsymbol{\beta})\right\}.  canonical pivotal alternative standard squared loss: makes gradient \boldsymbol{\beta} = 0 scale-free, PDB threshold require estimate noise standard deviation.

title: An introduction to `picreg`
text: family = "binomial" used logistic regression responses y_i \\{0, 1\} binary. case, pic requires y vector containing 0 1 values. format values y return error. picreg uses variance-stabilized transformation Bernoulli likelihood. \phi(\cdot) = \mathrm{Id} logistic link \theta_i = g(\eta_i) = (1 + e^{-\eta_i})^{-1}, pic(family = "binomial") minimizes  \min_{\beta_0, \boldsymbol\beta}\left\{ \frac{1}{n}\sum_{=1}^{n}\left(   2 y_i \sqrt{\tfrac{1 - \theta_i}{\theta_i}} + 2 (1 - y_i) \sqrt{\tfrac{\theta_i}{1 - \theta_i}} \right)+\lambda_\alpha^{\rm PBD}P(\boldsymbol\beta)\right\}, \qquad \theta_i = \frac{1}{1+\exp(-(\beta_0+\mathbf x_i^T\boldsymbol\beta))}  classical logistic link preserved; loss modified obtain pivotal gradient null. Binomial fits additionally support hard label prediction passing type = "class" predict() function.
code: data(BinomialExample) X <- BinomialExample$X y <- BinomialExample$y fit_binom <- pic(X, y, family = 'binomial') print(fit_binom) ## pic fit (pic.binomial) ##   family   : binomial ##   penalty  : lasso ##   lambda   : 0.191493 ##   selected : 5 / 50 ##   intercept: -0.281037 predict(fit_binom, newx = X[1:5, ], type = 'response') ## [1] 0.4193490 0.7368105 0.2277823 0.3310245 0.5189012 predict(fit_binom, newx = X[1:5, ], type = 'class') ## [1] 0 1 0 0 1

title: An introduction to `picreg`
text: count responses y_i \\mathbb{N}, pivotalization recipe applied Poisson likelihood. \phi(\cdot) = \mathrm{Id} canonical log link \theta_i = g(\eta_i) = e^{\eta_i}, pic(family = "poisson") minimizes  \min_{\beta_0, \boldsymbol\beta}\left\{ \frac{1}{n}\sum_{=1}^{n}\!\left(   \frac{2 y_i}{\sqrt{\theta_i}} + 2 \sqrt{\theta_i} \right)+\lambda_\alpha^{\rm PDB}P(\boldsymbol\beta)\right\}, \qquad \theta_i = \exp(\beta_0+\mathbf x_i^T\boldsymbol\beta).  canonical log link kept unchanged; pivotal property obtained loss rather link. pic requires y vector containing non negative values.

title: An introduction to `picreg`
text: standard Exponential negative log-likelihood used directly, transformation needed. \phi(\cdot) = \mathrm{Id} \theta_i = g(\eta_i) = e^{\eta_i}, pic(family = "exponential") minimizes  \min_{\beta_0, \boldsymbol\beta}\left\{ \frac{1}{n}\sum_{=1}^{n}\left(   \log{\theta_i} + \frac{y_i}{\theta_i} \right)+\lambda_\alpha^{\rm PDB}\right\}, \qquad \theta_i = \exp(\beta_0+\mathbf x_i^T\boldsymbol\beta).  , pic requires y vector containing non negative values.

title: An introduction to `picreg`
text: Gumbel distribution targets location-scale models extreme-value noise. base log-likelihood  l_n(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \sigma) = \log(\sigma) + \frac{1}{n}\sum_{=1}^{n} \bigl(z_i + e^{-z_i}\bigr), \qquad z_i = \frac{y_i - \theta_i}{\sigma}.  \phi(\cdot) = \exp(\cdot) identity link \theta_i = g(\eta_i) = \eta_i, optimization objective \phi\bigl(\ell_n(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \sigma; \mathcal{D})\bigr) = \exp\bigl(\ell_n(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \sigma)\bigr). scale parameter \sigma re-estimated internally maximum likelihood every iteration; user pass explicitly.

title: An introduction to `picreg`
text: Cox proportional-hazard model survival counterpart GLMs. response y must two-column matrix (t_i, \delta_i) event times event indicators (\delta_i = 1 event, 0 censored). \phi(\cdot) = \sqrt{\cdot} \theta_i = g(\eta_i) = \eta_i, pic(family = "cox") minimizes square-root normalized partial log-likelihood:  \min_{\boldsymbol\beta}\left\{ \sqrt{ -\frac1n\left( \sum_{=1}^{n} \delta_i \eta_i - \sum_{=1}^{n} \delta_i   \log\left(\sum_{j \R_i} e^{\eta_j}\right) \right)}+\lambda_\alpha^{\rm PDB}P(\boldsymbol\beta)\right\},  R_i denotes risk set event time t_i. Breslow approximation tied event times used. family = "cox" used, pic automatically fits model without intercept. package ships small survival dataset CoxExample (n = 250, p = 50, 5 active variables 45 noise variables) generated exponential proportional-hazards model independent exponential censoring. censoring rate roughly 40\%. response two-column matrix columns time event, standard input format family = "cox". addition shared interface, pic.cox objects carry Breslow estimates baseline cumulative hazard H_0(t) baseline survival S_0(t) = \exp\!\bigl(-H_0(t)\bigr), accessible directly fit_cox$baseline_cumulative_hazard fit_cox$baseline_survival. also visualized plot_baseline():  new design, predict_survival_function() returns common time grid matrix survival probabilities, one column per subject. result plotted directly plot_survival_curves():  visualize selected variable affects predicted survival curve, feature_effects_on_survival() evaluates model set “profile” subjects covariates fixed column means except chosen feature, varies across several values. default, function uses four empirical quantiles feature, distinct values small categorical ordinal variables. result can passed directly plot_survival_curves(). variables selected support can queried.  custom grid values can also supplied explicitly values argument:
code: data(CoxExample) X_cox <- CoxExample$X y_cox <- CoxExample$y print(y_cox[1:7, ]) ##             time event ## [1,] 0.005565264     1 ## [2,] 0.017211836     1 ## [3,] 0.017762667     1 ## [4,] 0.794925355     0 ## [5,] 0.008978275     1 ## [6,] 0.123304085     1 ## [7,] 0.179501892     0 fit_cox <- pic(X_cox, y_cox, family = "cox") print(fit_cox) ## pic fit (pic.cox) ##   family   : cox ##   penalty  : lasso ##   lambda   : 0.0476666 ##   selected : 5 / 50 plot_baseline(fit_cox) sf <- predict_survival_function(fit_cox, newx = X_cox[1:3, ]) plot_survival_curves(sf) fx <- feature_effects_on_survival(fit_cox, idx = "gene_1") plot_survival_curves(fx) fx <- feature_effects_on_survival(fit_cox,                                   idx    = "gene_3",                                   values = c(-2, -1, 0, 1, 2)) plot_survival_curves(fx)

what: A note on bias and refitting
title: An introduction to `picreg`
text: word caution interpreting predictive metrics: default pic() called relax = FALSE, reported coefficients still carry shrinkage induced penalty. particularly pronounced LASSO (penalty = "lasso"), whose bias non-zero coefficients vanish even signal grows. SCAD MCP reduce effect residual bias still non-negligible moderate signals. goal prediction quality, safest pattern refit selected support without penalization. Two ways: Call pic() relax = TRUE. regularized path converges, unpenalized refit run selected variables, yielding debiased coefficients immediately usable prediction. Manually re-fit subset X[, fit$selected] estimator choice.
code: fit_relax <- pic(X, y, relax = TRUE) assess(fit_relax, newx = X, newy = y) ##  metric     value ##     MSE 0.7631608 ##     MAE 0.6993721 ##      R2 0.8284080

title: An introduction to `picreg`
text: widely used package penalized linear regression R ecosystem glmnet (Friedman, Hastie & Tibshirani). glmnet chooses \lambda cross-validation, requires fitting full regularization path refitting CV-selected value - typically 100 fits. picreg selects \lambda_\alpha^{\mathrm{PDB}} closed form design alone fits . fundamentally, cross-validation tunes \lambda minimize predictive error, objective recovering true support; picreg instead calibrates \lambda specifically support recovery, therefore tends recover active set accurately. two targets fundamentally different - picreg’s answer closer one practitioners actually want ask “feature selection”

what: Support recovery on a small Gaussian benchmark
title: An introduction to `picreg`
text: direct illustration sparse Gaussian design. simulate n = 100 observations, p = 100 covariates, s = 5 truly active variables (first five) carrying coefficient 3 independent standard-Gaussian noise: Three selectors run single draw: picreg (\ell_1 \widehat{\lambda}^{\mathrm{PDB}}), cv.glmnet prediction-optimal lambda.min, cv.glmnet sparser lambda.1se heuristic. small simulation, picreg typically recovers five true variables exactly. cv.glmnet recovers , handful additional spurious selections - well-documented behavior CV-tuned \ell_1: chosen lambda.min minimizes prediction error, usually sits support-recovery threshold, noise features sneak . Using lambda.1se generally produces sparser model fewer false positives. However, choice remains somewhat ad hoc: heuristic designed favor parsimony rather principled calibration specifically targeting support recovery.
code: set.seed(1) n <- 100; p <- 100; s <- 5 X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p) beta <- numeric(p) beta[1:s] <- 3 y <- as.numeric(X %*% beta + rnorm(n)) true_support <- seq_len(s) rbind(   cbind(method = "picreg (lasso)",         metrics(sel_pic, true_support)),   cbind(method = "cv.glmnet (lambda.min)", metrics(sel_min, true_support)),   cbind(method = "cv.glmnet (lambda.1se)", metrics(sel_1se, true_support)) ) ##                   method selected exact_recovery ## 1         picreg (lasso)        5           TRUE ## 2 cv.glmnet (lambda.min)       17          FALSE ## 3 cv.glmnet (lambda.1se)        7          FALSE

title: An introduction to `picreg`
text: common (reasonable) concern leaving glmnet runtime. honest summary first, benchmarks. picreg, actual fit (FISTA path) fast - typically faster glmnet, glmnet performs K-fold cross-validation hood thus fits model order K \times n_\lambda times (typically K=10 folds \times n_\lambda=100-point grid). dominant cost picreg fact computation \lambda_\alpha^{\mathrm{PDB}} , scales n: n moderate (say thousand), even accurate "mc_exact" selector cheap whole pipeline finishes fast. n becomes large, "mc_exact" Monte Carlo dominates runtime. regime, switching "mc_gaussian" "analytical" gives essentially \widehat{\lambda} tiny fraction cost (see Asymptotic behavior selector section), picreg remains competitive - often faster full CV pass. Gaussian designs, glmnet fast. Fortran coordinate-descent backend squared loss extremely well optimized. picreg runs FISTA square-root LASSO loss, structurally expensive per iteration (closed-form per-coordinate step). Gaussian benchmarks picreg therefore slower per fit, although CV overhead glmnet side closes gap end--end “fit + select” comparison. distributions/families, difference shrinks even flips: glmnet’s coordinate descent non-Gaussian likelihoods requires inner Newton-IRLS iterations, picreg’s FISTA handles single sweep. Combined absence CV, picreg usually competitive faster.
code: X <- matrix(rnorm(1e4 * 200), 1e4, 200) Y <- rnorm(1e4) system.time(pic(X, Y)) ##    user  system elapsed  ##   1.238   0.104   1.404 system.time(pic(X, Y, lambda_method = "analytical")) ##    user  system elapsed  ##   0.188   0.023   0.212 system.time(cv.glmnet(X, Y)) ##    user  system elapsed  ##   0.998   0.052   1.054 X <- matrix(rnorm(1e4 * 200), 1e4, 200) Y <- rbinom(1e4, size = 1, prob = 0.5) system.time(pic(X, Y, family = "binomial")) ##    user  system elapsed  ##   1.805   0.115   1.941 system.time(pic(X, Y, family = "binomial", lambda_method = "analytical")) ##    user  system elapsed  ##   0.189   0.023   0.213 system.time(cv.glmnet(X, Y, family = "binomial")) ##    user  system elapsed  ##   3.191   0.084   3.377

what: Variable Selection using the Pivotal Information Criterion
title: Variable Selection using the Pivotal Information Criterion
text: Variable selection using Pivotal Information Criterion. Sparse regression classification via Pivotal Information Criterion (PIC), alternative BIC, cross-validation, Lasso-based tuning. regularization parameter selected pivotal null-distribution statistic, eliminating need cross-validation yielding sharper support recovery. Provides FISTA optimization L1, SCAD, MCP penalties across six response distributions: standard sparsity assumptions, selector achieves phase transition exact support recovery, analogous results compressed sensing.

title: Assess performance of a pic fit. — assess
text: metrics depend family: "gaussian" MSE, MAE, R-squared. "binomial" accuracy, AUC, binomial deviance. "poisson" MSE, MAE, Poisson deviance. "exponential" MSE, MAE, Exponential deviance. text: Computes data-driven regularization parameter \(\hat\lambda^{\rm PDB}_\alpha\) using Pivotal Detection Boundary (PDB) principle. selected value defined empirical \((1 - \alpha)\) quantile null-distribution gradient statistic $$\hat\lambda^{\rm PDB}_\alpha =q_{1-\alpha}\left(\left\|\nabla \ell_0\right\|_\infty \right),$$ \(\ell_0\) denotes loss evaluated null model.

Can either family object character string accepted get_family(). n_simu Number Monte Carlo simulations used stochastic methods. Ignored method = "analytical". alpha Nominal tail probability used define quantile level. method One "mc_exact", "mc_gaussian", "analytical".

title: Pivotal Detection Boundary regularization selector — lambda_pdb
text: object class "pic.lambda_pdb". value Selected regularization parameter \(\hat\lambda^{\rm PDB}_\alpha\). statistics Simulated null statistics used estimate quantile. NULL analytical method. method Estimation method used. alpha Quantile level parameter. n_simu Number Monte Carlo simulations.

title: Pivotal Detection Boundary regularization selector — lambda_pdb
text: null \(\beta = 0\), gradient loss carries sampling noise. smallest \(\lambda\) large enough dominate noise natural threshold separating signal noise, .e. value coefficient kept rather shrunk zero. Calibrating \(\hat\lambda\) way two consequences. First, quantile depends design matrix \(X\), family, level \(\alpha\) (response \(y\)), cross-validation longer needed. Second, importantly, leads sharper support recovery prediction-error-based selectors cross-validated lasso: targeting noise level gradient directly, PDB controls inclusion noise variables reliably identifies true non-zero coefficients. Computing quantile requires distribution \(\|\nabla \ell_0\|_\infty\), lambda_pdb() estimates one three methods .

title: Pivotal Detection Boundary regularization selector — lambda_pdb
text: empirical quantile obtained using one three following methods: "mc_exact" Family-aware Monte Carlo. n_simu draws, response vector sampled null model (\(\beta = 0\)) chosen family; family-specific gradient loss \(\beta = 0\) evaluated fixed design \(X\), supremum norm recorded. empirical \((1 - \alpha)\) quantile n_simu recorded norms returned. accurate slowest three. "mc_gaussian" Monte Carlo Gaussian approximation null gradient. central-limit argument gives \(\nabla \ell_0 \approx \mathcal{N}(0,\, c(n)\, \Sigma_X / n)\) \(\Sigma_X = X^\top X / n\). n_simu draws samples directly Gaussian records supremum norm — family-specific evaluation needed. Family-agnostic noticeably faster "mc_exact"; valid regime CLT kicks (moderate large \(n\)). "analytical" Closed-form Bonferroni bound Gaussian tail. Combining union bound \(p\) coordinates gradient standard Gaussian tail bound gives $$\hat\lambda_\alpha^{\rm analytical} =           \Phi^{-1}\!\left(1 - \alpha / (2p)\right)\, \sqrt{c(n) / n}.$$ Deterministic \(O(1)\) — simulation. Conservative (slightly -estimates true quantile) gets looser \(p\) grows, useful speed matters Monte Carlo overkill. "mc_gaussian" "analytical" methods use variance scaling factor \(c(n)\) depending family: Gaussian / Binomial / Poisson / Exponential: \(c(n) = 1\) Gumbel: \(c(n) = \exp(2(\gamma + 1))\), \(\gamma\) Euler-Mascheroni constant. Cox: \(c(n) = 1 / (4 \log n)\)

title: Pivotal Detection Boundary regularization selector — lambda_pdb
text: Monte Carlo methods dominated single \(p \times n_{\rm simu}\) matrix product \(X^\top R\), \(R\) stacks simulated residuals. product dispatched BLAS single \(\tt gemm\), essentially fast Monte Carlo selector can dense designs. large \(n\) \(p\), however, constant becomes large "mc_exact" may unnecessarily expensive: \(n\) grows, central-limit approximation tightens "mc_gaussian" gives essentially \(\hat\lambda\) "mc_exact" fraction cost (family-specific residual generation, fewer dependencies \(y\)-draws). "analytical" \(O(1)\) useful quick upper bound — slightly conservative, accurate enough triage high \(p\) Bonferroni tail tight. Practical rule thumb: prefer "mc_exact" small moderate problems (default), "mc_gaussian" \(n\) grows design dense, "analytical" even Monte Carlo cost concern.

title: Pivotal Detection Boundary regularization selector — lambda_pdb
text: level \(\alpha\) nominal Type-error test \(H_0\!\colon \beta = 0\). construction quantile, $$\Pr\!\left(\left\|\nabla \ell_0\right\|_\infty   > \hat\lambda^{\rm PDB}_\alpha \,\big|\, H_0\right) = \alpha,$$ null model variable enters active set probability \(\alpha\). default \(\alpha = 0.05\), caps false-discovery rate \(5\%\) null: data carry signal, picreg returns empty support \(95\%\) time.

title: Phase-transition analysis of support recovery. — phase_transition
text: Monte Carlo replicate design matrix drawn standard Gaussian, s features sampled uniformly random, response generated chosen type, one pic fit run requested penalty. selected support compared truth three metrics stored: exact_recovery 1 selected set equals true set, 0 otherwise. tpr \(\left|\hat{S}\cap S\right| / |S|\) - true positive rate. s = 0, set 1 true feature exists. fdr \(\left|\hat{S}\setminus S\right| / \max{(|\hat{S}|, 1)}\) - false discovery rate.

title: Phase-transition analysis of support recovery. — phase_transition
text: replicate, design \(X\) drawn iid \(\mathcal{N}(0, 1)\), true support \(S\) sampled uniformly random, \(\beta_j = \) beta_value \(j \S\), \(\beta_j = 0\) otherwise. linear predictor \(\eta = X\beta\). response \(y\) drawn conditionally \(\eta\) according requested family: "gaussian" \(y_i = \eta_i + \varepsilon_i\) \(\varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\). "binomial" \(y_i \sim \mathrm{Bernoulli}\!\left(\sigma(\eta_i)\right)\), \(\sigma(z) = 1 / (1 + e^{-z})\) logistic function. "poisson" \(y_i \sim \mathrm{Poisson}\!\left(e^{\eta_i}\right)\). "exponential" \(y_i \sim \mathrm{Exp}\!\left(\mathrm{rate} = e^{\eta_i}\right)\). "gumbel" \(y_i = \eta_i + \varepsilon_i\) \(\varepsilon_i \sim \mathrm{Gumbel}(0, 1)\), drawn \(-\log(-\log U_i)\) \(U_i \sim \mathcal{U}(0, 1)\). "cox" Event times \(T_i \sim \mathrm{Exp}\!\left(e^{\eta_i}\right)\) independent censoring times \(C_i \sim \mathrm{Exp}(1)\). response 2-column matrix \(\bigl(\min(T_i, C_i),\, \mathbf{1}\{T_i \le C_i\}\bigr)\).

title: Sparse linear regression using the Pivotal Information Criterion. — pic
text: Minimises objective function $$\hat\beta = \arg\min_\beta\; L(\beta)        \;+\; \lambda_\alpha^{\rm PDB}\,\mathrm{pen}(\beta)        \quad\text{}\quad        L(\beta) = \phi\!\left(\ell_n\!\left(g(\beta)\right)\right),$$ regularization parameter selected automatically using Pivotal Detection Boundary (PDB) principle implemented lambda_pdb(). PDB choice just substitute cross-validation: calibrating \(\lambda\) pivotal null-distribution statistic rather --sample prediction error, yields sharper support recovery - fewer false positives noise variables accurate identification true non-zero coefficients. , \(\phi\) \(g\) family-specific transformations chosen gradient null distribution free nuisance parameter, precisely makes use \(\lambda^{\rm PDB}_\alpha\) valid. Optimization performed using warm-started FISTA regularization path.

title: Sparse linear regression using the Pivotal Information Criterion. — pic
text: "gaussian" Uses mean squared error \(\ell_n\), \(\phi(\cdot) = \sqrt{\cdot}\) \(g(\cdot) = \mathrm{Id}\), giving square-root lasso loss $$L(\beta) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{=1}^n       \left(y_i - (\beta_0 + \mathbf{x}_i^\top\beta)\right)^2}.$$ makes gradient \(\beta = 0\) scale-free, PDB threshold needs estimate noise standard deviation. "binomial" variance-stabilized transformation Bernoulli likelihood. \(\phi(\cdot) = \mathrm{Id}\) logistic link \(\theta_i = g(\eta_i) = (1 + e^{-\eta_i})^{-1}\), loss $$L(\beta) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{=1}^n       \left(2 y_i \sqrt{\frac{1 - \theta_i}{\theta_i}}             + 2 (1 - y_i) \sqrt{\frac{\theta_i}{1 - \theta_i}}\right).$$ classical logistic link preserved; loss modified obtain pivotal gradient null. "poisson" pivotalization applied Poisson likelihood. \(\phi(\cdot) = \mathrm{Id}\) canonical log link \(\theta_i = g(\eta_i) = e^{\eta_i}\), loss $$L(\beta) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{=1}^n       \left(\frac{2 y_i}{\sqrt{\theta_i}} + 2 \sqrt{\theta_i}\right).$$ canonical log link kept unchanged; pivotality obtained loss rather link. "exponential" Uses standard Exponential negative log-likelihood directly (transformation). \(\phi(\cdot) = \mathrm{Id}\) \(\theta_i = g(\eta_i) = e^{\eta_i}\), loss $$L(\beta) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{=1}^n       \left(\log\theta_i + \frac{y_i}{\theta_i}\right).$$ "gumbel" location-scale model extreme-value noise. base log-likelihood $$\ell_n(\theta, \sigma) = \log(\sigma)       + \frac{1}{n}\sum_{=1}^n \left(z_i + e^{-z_i}\right),       \qquad z_i = \frac{y_i - \theta_i}{\sigma}.$$ \(\phi(\cdot) = \exp(\cdot)\) identity link \(\theta_i = g(\eta_i) = \eta_i\), objective \(L(\beta, \sigma) = \exp(\ell_n(\theta, \sigma))\). scale \(\sigma\) re-estimated internally maximum likelihood every iteration; user supply . "cox" square-root-transformed Cox partial log-likelihood. \(\phi(\cdot) = \sqrt{\cdot}\) identity link \(\theta_i = g(\eta_i) = \eta_i\), objective $$L(\beta) = \sqrt{-\frac{1}{n}\left(       \sum_{=1}^n \delta_i \eta_i       - \sum_{=1}^n \delta_i         \log\left(\sum_{j \R_i} e^{\eta_j}\right)\right)},$$ \(\delta_i\) event indicator \(R_i\) risk set event time \(t_i\). Breslow approximation used tied event times, model always fitted without intercept.

title: Sparsity-inducing penalties for pic. — pic_penalties
text: Three penalties supported, identified lowercase name match C++ registry. penalty enters pic() objective $$\mathrm{pen}(\beta) = \sum_{j=1}^p p_\lambda(|\beta_j|),$$ \(p_\lambda(\cdot)\) depends penalty.

title: Sparsity-inducing penalties for pic. — pic_penalties
text: "lasso" L1 (soft-thresholding) penalty: $$p_\lambda(|t|) = \lambda |t|.$$ Convex, gives strongest shrinkage large coefficients bias vanish \(|t| \\infty\). "scad" (Smoothly Clipped Absolute Deviation, Fan & Li 2001) Non-convex penalty concavity parameter scad_a > 2 (default 3.7): $$p_\lambda'(|t|) = \lambda\!\left\{        \mathbf{1}\{|t| \le \lambda\}        + \frac{(\lambda - |t|)_+}{(- 1)\lambda}          \mathbf{1}\{|t| > \lambda\}\right\}.$$ Behaves like lasso small \(|t|\), tapers large coefficients barely penalized - yields nearly unbiased estimates strong signals. "mcp" (Minimax Concave Penalty, Zhang 2010) Non-convex penalty concavity parameter mcp_gamma > 1 (default 3.0): $$p_\lambda'(|t|) = \left(\lambda - \frac{|t|}{\gamma}\right)_+.$$ Similar motivation SCAD smoother transition: starts lasso derivative small \(|t|\) tapers linearly zero \(|t| = \gamma\lambda\). actual evaluation proximal operators live C++ (src/penalty_*.cpp). Larger scad_a / mcp_gamma make penalty closer lasso; smaller values amplify non-convexity (bias reduction strong signals).

what: picreg: Variable Selection using the Pivotal Information Criterion — picreg-package
title: picreg: Variable Selection using the Pivotal Information Criterion — picreg-package
text: Sparse regression classification via Pivotal Information Criterion (PIC), alternative Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC), cross-validation, Lasso-based tuning. regularization parameter selected pivotal null-distribution statistic, eliminating need cross-validation yielding sharper support recovery. Provides Fast Iterative Shrinkage-Thresholding Algorithm (FISTA) optimization L1, Smoothly Clipped Absolute Deviation (SCAD), Minimax Concave Penalty (MCP) penalties across six response distributions: Gaussian, binomial, Poisson, exponential, Gumbel, Cox. standard sparsity assumptions, selector achieves phase transition exact support recovery, analogous results compressed sensing. See Sardy, van Cutsem van de Geer (2026) doi:10.48550/arXiv.2603.04172 .

title: picreg 0.1.3
text: Resolved C++ One Definition Rule (ODR) violation reported link-time optimization (LTO): file-local helper structs fista.cpp fista_cox.cpp now internal linkage (anonymous namespaces).

title: picreg 0.1.3
text: coef() now returns one-column sparse matrix instead two-column data frame. Zero coefficients printed ., keeps display compact high dimensions. Added summary() method pic fits. intercept = FALSE now permitted every family. Previously raised error non-Cox families standardize = TRUE. intercept = FALSE, data assumed centered. Standardized input-validation error messages (consistent back-quoting argument names call. = FALSE).
